hook: You already know how to graph lines, and you've seen quadratic expressions like $x^2$ show up in equations. But lines only go in one direction — what happens when a relationship curves? In this unit, you'll discover how a single equation can produce a graceful U-shaped curve that models everything from a basketball's arc to the shape of satellite dishes.
concept: Every topic in this unit builds toward understanding one powerful curve: the parabola. All the graphs you'll study are variations of the quadratic function $y = ax^2 + bx + c$, and your goal is to see how each part of that equation controls the shape, position, and direction of the curve.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn what a parabola is, where it appears in the real world, and what key vocabulary — like vertex, axis of symmetry, and direction of opening — you'll use throughout the unit.
Master how the coefficient $a$ controls whether a parabola opens up or down and whether it appears narrow or wide compared to the parent graph $y = x^2$.
Apply your understanding of vertical shifts to graph parabolas that slide up or down based on the value of $c$, without changing their shape.
You will learn to graph the full quadratic function by identifying its vertex and plotting key points to produce an accurate parabola.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Finding the Vertex and Axis of Symmetry
topic_roadmap.description: Master using the formula $x = -\dfrac{b}{2a}$ to precisely locate the vertex and axis of symmetry for any quadratic function.
You will learn to read a parabola's graph and identify all its important features — including the vertex, axis of symmetry, intercepts, domain, and range.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Comparing Linear, Quadratic, and Exponential Graphs
topic_roadmap.description: Apply what you've learned to distinguish quadratic graphs from linear and exponential ones, explaining how their shapes, rates of change, and equations differ.
